senses.definition: Preventing or counteracting polyneuritis (inflammation of many peripheral nerves).
examples: The discovery of antipolyneuritic factors helped reduce nerve disorders caused by vitamin deficiencies.
Researchers tested an antipolyneuritic compound in patients with symptoms of polyneuritis.
Adequate intake of certain vitamins has a strong antipolyneuritic effect in at‑risk populations.

senses.definition: Opposing or banning pooling agreements, especially between rival railroads.
examples: Several states passed antipool laws in the late nineteenth century.
The commission took an antipool position to protect competition among carriers.
Antipool regulations limited the power of railway trusts at that time.

senses.definition: Opposing or banning pooling agreements, especially associations of rival railroads to fix rates or share profits.
examples: Antipooling measures were introduced to curb monopoly power in the railway industry.
The senator supported antipooling legislation to protect small shippers.
Antipooling rules changed how railroads set freight charges.

senses.definition: A person who makes a serious claim to be pope in opposition to the pope accepted by the Roman Catholic Church.
examples: During the Western Schism, more than one antipope claimed the papal throne.
The council declared him an antipope and removed his authority.
Historians still debate how many antipopes there have been.

senses.definition: Strong opposition or hostility to the papacy or to Roman Catholicism, especially in older political or religious debates.
examples: Pamphlets full of antipopery helped shape public opinion in eighteenth‑century England.
The speech was marked by fierce antipopery and anti‑Catholic stereotypes.
Antipopery played an important role in some early American political movements.

senses.definition: Plural of antipope.
examples: Several antipopes appeared during the church crises of the Middle Ages.
The historian carefully lists all known antipopes and their supporters.
Conflicts between popes and antipopes often divided European rulers.

senses.definition: Not popular with the general public; going against what most people like or support.
examples: The government introduced several antipopular measures during the crisis.
Her antipopular views made her a controversial figure.
Raising taxes was an antipopular but necessary decision.

senses.definition: Opposition to the popularization of something, especially a belief, product, or cultural form.
examples: The movement’s antipopularization reflects a fear of losing its original identity.
Some critics show clear antipopularization in their attacks on mass culture.
Antipopularization can limit how widely scientific ideas are shared.

senses.definition: A person who opposes population growth or supports policies to limit population size.
examples: As an antipopulationist, he warned of the impact of overpopulation on resources.
Antipopulationists often argue for strict family‑planning policies.
The conference brought together environmentalists and antipopulationists.

senses.definition: Relating to opposition to population growth.
examples: The party’s antipopulationist policy focused on strict birth control.
Antipopulationist writings became influential in some policy circles.
Their antipopulationist stance worried business leaders.

senses.definition: Opposition to populism or to populist political movements and leaders.
examples: Her book explores the rise of antipopulism among urban elites.
Antipopulism can be seen in the media’s reaction to grassroots movements.
The party’s antipopulism alienated many working‑class voters.

senses.definition: Opposed to pornography or to its production, sale, or use.
examples: Several antiporn groups protested outside the video store.
The city introduced antiporn laws targeting adult theaters.
She joined an antiporn campaign after studying media effects.

senses.definition: Opposing pornography or designed to restrict or criticize pornography.
examples: The organization publishes antipornographic materials for schools.
Antipornographic campaigns often focus on protecting children.
They supported antipornographic laws in the national parliament.

senses.definition: Opposition to pornography and efforts to reduce or ban it.
examples: The group is known for its strong antipornography activism.
Antipornography campaigns have influenced local laws.
Her research examines the history of antipornography in feminist movements.

senses.definition: Not portable; not easy or not intended to be carried or moved.
examples: The machine’s antiportable design keeps it fixed in one place.
Such antiportable equipment is installed permanently in the lab.
Because it is antiportable, the unit must be serviced on site.

senses.definition: A position or viewpoint that is directly opposed to another position.
examples: He took an antiposition to the government’s economic plan.
The article clearly states the main argument and the antiposition.
Students were asked to present the antiposition in the debate.

senses.definition: Opposed to the use, sale, or legalization of marijuana.
examples: Antipot activists campaigned against the new legalization bill.
The school has a strictly antipot policy on campus.
Her antipot stance comes from working with addicted teenagers.

senses.definition: Intended to reduce or fight poverty.
examples: The government launched a new antipoverty program in rural areas.
Antipoverty measures focused on education and healthcare.
Several charities work together on antipoverty initiatives.

senses.definition: Opposed to a pragmatic or practical approach, especially in politics, law, or philosophy.
examples: Some judges criticize this theory as too moralistic and antipragmatic.
Her antipragmatic view rejects solutions based only on what works in practice.
The article attacks what it sees as an antipragmatic attitude in legal reasoning.

senses.definition: Having an antipragmatic character; not guided by practical results.
examples: He dismissed their antipragmatical refusal to compromise.
Such antipragmatical ideas rarely succeed in real politics.
The policy was criticized as unrealistic and antipragmatical.

senses.definition: In a way that is not pragmatic or practical, often by rejecting results‑based thinking.
examples: The law was interpreted antipragmatically, with little concern for real‑world effects.
They argued antipragmatically, focusing on pure principles.
The plan, though noble, was developed antipragmatically and soon failed.

senses.definition: A general opposition to pragmatism as a philosophy or method.
examples: His antipragmaticism leads him to reject policies based purely on outcomes.
Antipragmaticism appears in critiques of experimental legal approaches.
The book traces the roots of antipragmaticism in modern theology.

senses.definition: Opposition to pragmatism, especially as a philosophical or legal approach.
examples: Antipragmatism in the courts can slow legal reform.
The article criticizes antipragmatism in public policy debates.
His antipragmatism makes him suspicious of trial‑and‑error methods.

senses.definition: A person who rejects or strongly criticizes pragmatism.
examples: As an antipragmatist, she distrusts policies justified only by results.
The debate between the pragmatist and the antipragmatist lasted for hours.
Many antipragmatists fear that practical thinking ignores moral duties.

senses.definition: A substance, usually an antibody, that counteracts or prevents the action of a precipitin.
examples: The reaction was blocked by adding an antiprecipitin to the serum.
Researchers measured antiprecipitin activity in the sample.
The presence of antiprecipitin complicates interpretation of the test.

senses.definition: Designed to protect an organism from predators or to reduce the risk of being eaten.
examples: Many animals show antipredator behavior such as staying in groups.
Bright colors can serve as an antipredator signal.
The study focused on antipredator strategies in birds.

senses.definition: Defensive agents or mechanisms that protect against predators.
examples: Chemical antipredators in the skin make the frog taste bad.
The plant relies on thorns as natural antipredators.
Researchers studied how antipredators influence predator behavior.

senses.definition: A factor or element that works against a particular predetermined outcome.
examples: Social mobility can act as an antipredeterminant of inherited class status.
Education is often described as an antipredeterminant of poverty.
The study identified several antipredeterminants that weaken genetic influence.

senses.definition: To oppose or act against church prelates or high clergy.
examples: Reformers began to antiprelate openly, criticizing the power of bishops.
Those who antiprelated risked charges of heresy.
He continued to antiprelate in his writings despite official warnings.

senses.definition: Opposed to prelates or to church government by high‑ranking clergy.
examples: Antiprelatic writers attacked the privileges of bishops.
The movement had a strong antiprelatic and republican tone.
His antiprelatic views led him to support simpler church structures.

senses.definition: Opposition to rule or control by church prelates.
examples: Antiprelatism shaped many Puritan arguments about church government.
The pamphlet is a classic statement of seventeenth‑century antiprelatism.
Antiprelatism often went together with demands for political liberty.

senses.definition: A person who opposes rule or influence by church prelates.
examples: The antiprelatists demanded a more democratic church structure.
As an antiprelatist, he criticized bishops in his sermons.
Many antiprelatists supported broader political reforms as well.

senses.definition: Opposition to military preparedness or to building up armed forces in advance of war.
examples: Antipreparedness was strong among pacifist groups before the war.
The speech reflects a clear antipreparedness in foreign policy.
Public antipreparedness faded after the first attacks.

senses.definition: Hostile or opposed to the press or news media.
examples: The leader’s antipress attitude worried free‑speech advocates.
Antipress statements damaged the government’s image abroad.
His antipress remarks led to tension with journalists.

senses.definition: Opposition to prestidigitation, that is, to magic tricks or sleight‑of‑hand performances.
examples: Religious leaders expressed antiprestidigitation, calling such shows deceptive.
The era’s antiprestidigitation limited public magic performances.
His antiprestidigitation came from a belief that tricks encouraged superstition.

senses.definition: Strongly opposed to priests or priestly influence.
examples: The pamphlet contains harsh antipriest language.
Antipriest feelings grew during the revolution.
His antipriest views made him unpopular with church authorities.

senses.definition: Opposition to priestcraft, that is, to the power or secret influence of priests.
examples: Their writings are full of Enlightenment‑style antipriestcraft.
Antipriestcraft led many thinkers to call for separation of church and state.
The movement combined rationalism with strong antipriestcraft.

senses.definition: Hostility toward the institution or class of priests.
examples: Revolutionary antipriesthood fueled attacks on church property.
The novel reflects a deep antipriesthood in its portrayal of clergy.
Antipriesthood combined with economic anger to spark unrest.

senses.definition: A positive integer with more divisors than any smaller positive integer.
examples: Number theorists note that 24 is an antiprime.
The sequence of antiprimes grows quite slowly.
Students were asked to list the first five antiprimes.

senses.definition: A cartridge case or device designed to reduce primer sensitivity or to prevent accidental firing.
examples: The new antiprimer design increases firearm safety.
Engineers tested an antiprimer to stop misfires during transport.
The antiprimer helps prevent accidental discharge if the weapon is dropped.

senses.definition: The process of reducing primer sensitivity or making something less ready to ignite or activate.
examples: Antipriming techniques are used to prevent accidental explosions.
The study examined antipriming in different primer compositions.
Engineers improved antipriming without affecting performance.

senses.definition: A principle that directly contradicts or negates another principle.
examples: Free speech can sometimes clash with the antiprinciple of strict social control.
They argued that his proposal rested on an antiprinciple to democracy.
An antiprinciple emerged in reaction to the original doctrine.

senses.definition: A solid figure with two identical polygonal faces placed one above the other and joined by a band of triangles.
examples: The octagonal antiprism has eight‑sided top and bottom faces.
Students built models of an antiprism using paper triangles.
The antiprism is a common example in solid geometry.

senses.definition: A person who opposes increased production, especially industrial or economic production, often for environmental or social reasons.
examples: Antiproductionists warn that endless growth is unsustainable.
The conference brought together antiproductionists and climate activists.
He has been called an antiproductionist because he questions mass manufacturing.

senses.definition: Opposing increased or mass production.
examples: Their antiproductionist policy favors small‑scale local industry.
Antiproductionist ideas challenge mainstream economic thinking.
The party’s platform contains several antiproductionist proposals.

senses.definition: Having the opposite effect to what is intended and reducing productivity or success.
examples: Excessive control can be antiproductive in creative work.
Long, unfocused meetings are often antiproductive.
The new rules turned out to be antiproductive for the team.

senses.definition: In a way that reduces productivity or works against the desired result.
examples: They responded antiproductively by blaming each other.
Time was used antiproductively during the meeting.
The team worked antiproductively under unclear leadership.

senses.definition: The quality of reducing productivity or working against successful results.
examples: The antiproductiveness of constant interruptions is obvious.
Managers must recognize antiproductiveness in team routines.
Her research looks at antiproductiveness in office communication.

senses.definition: A tendency or set of conditions that reduce productivity.
examples: Open‑plan offices can create antiproductivity by increasing noise.
The report analyzes antiproductivity in remote work settings.
Antiproductivity rises when goals are not clearly defined.

senses.definition: Aimed at preventing unfair or excessive profits, especially in times of crisis or war.
examples: The government introduced antiprofiteering laws during the shortage.
Antiprofiteering measures targeted companies raising prices sharply.
An antiprofiteering clause was added to the contract.

senses.definition: Opposed to progressive ideas, changes, or political movements.
examples: The proposal faced strong antiprogressive criticism.
Antiprogressive forces blocked the education reform.
His antiprogressive stance appeals to traditional voters.

senses.definition: Opposition to prohibition laws, especially those banning alcohol or other substances.
examples: Antiprohibition grew as people saw the rise of illegal trade.
The antiprohibition movement eventually led to a change in the law.
Newspapers reported on rallies full of antiprohibition slogans.
